title.emphasis: Part V: The Freedoms of Assembly and Petition

section.para: The final two freedoms guaranteed by the First Amendment—the right of the people "peaceably to assemble" and to "petition the Government for a redress of grievances"—are essential to collective action and democratic accountability. The Court's jurisprudence has recognized an implicit "freedom of association" and has developed doctrines to protect protests and gatherings in public spaces, while also affirming that litigation itself can be a form of protected petition.

title.emphasis: A. The Right of Expressive and Intimate Association

section.para: While not explicitly mentioned in the First Amendment, the Supreme Court has recognized the freedom of association as an indispensable means of preserving other explicit freedoms, such as speech and religion.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
NAACP v. Alabama ex rel. Patterson **, 357 U.S. 449 (1958)**
section.para: In an effort to oust the NAACP from the state, Alabama sought a court order compelling the organization to disclose its membership lists.117 The Supreme Court unanimously held that this demand was unconstitutional.118 The Court recognized that "inviolability of privacy in group association may in many circumstances be indispensable to preservation of freedom of association".117 It found that compelled disclosure of the NAACP's members would expose them to "economic reprisal, loss of employment, threat of physical coercion, and other manifestations of public hostility," thereby chilling their right to freely associate.117 This case established freedom of association as a fundamental right protected by the Fourteenth Amendment.117

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Roberts v. United States Jaycees **, 468 U.S. 609 (1984)**
section.para: This case tested the limits of associational freedom when pitted against anti-discrimination laws. The U.S. Jaycees, a civic organization that limited full membership to men, was challenged under a Minnesota law that prohibited gender discrimination in public accommodations.122 The Supreme Court unanimously held that Minnesota's compelling interest in eradicating discrimination against women justified applying the law to the Jaycees.122 The Court reasoned that the Jaycees was a large and "basically unselective" group, and forcing it to admit women would not impose any serious burden on the male members' freedom of expressive association.122

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Boy Scouts of America v. Dale **, 530 U.S. 640 (2000)**
section.para: In a decision that contrasted sharply with Roberts, the Court upheld the Boy Scouts of America's right to exclude an openly gay assistant scoutmaster, James Dale.126 The Court, in a 5-4 decision, held that forcing the BSA to accept Dale would violate the organization's First Amendment right of expressive association.126 The majority found that the BSA's opposition to homosexual conduct was part of its "expressive message" and that Dale's presence as a scoutmaster would "at the very least, force the organization to send a message... that the Boy Scouts accept homosexual conduct as a legitimate form of behavior".126 The ruling affirmed that a private organization may exclude a member when that person's presence "affects in a significant way the group's ability to advocate public or private viewpoints".126

title.emphasis: B. The Public Forum: The Right to Protest and Assemble

section.para: The right to assemble peaceably is most often exercised in public spaces. The Court has developed a body of law, known as the public forum doctrine, to govern when, where, and how citizens can use public property for expressive purposes.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Hague v. CIO **, 307 U.S. 496 (1939)**
section.para: The Committee for Industrial Organization (CIO) was prevented from holding labor meetings in public places in Jersey City under an ordinance that gave the Director of Safety unfettered discretion to grant or deny permits.128 The Supreme Court invalidated the ordinance.128 In a plurality opinion, Justice Owen Roberts famously declared that public streets and parks "have immemorially been held in trust for the use of the public and, time out of mind, have been used for purposes of assembly, communicating thoughts between citizens, and discussing public questions".128 This case established the foundational concept of the traditional public forum, where speech and assembly rights are at their zenith.6

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Cox v. New Hampshire **, 312 U.S. 569 (1941)**
section.para: A group of Jehovah's Witnesses were convicted for holding a parade on a public street without first obtaining a special license as required by a state statute.130 The Supreme Court unanimously upheld their convictions, finding the statute constitutional.131 The Court established that the government can impose reasonable "time, place, and manner" restrictions on speech and assembly in public forums.130 Such regulations are permissible provided they are content-neutral, are narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest (like public safety and order), and leave open ample alternative channels for communication.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Edwards v. South Carolina **, 372 U.S. 229 (1963)**
section.para: A group of 187 Black students peacefully marched to the South Carolina State House grounds to protest segregation.132 They were arrested for breach of the peace after they refused a police order to disperse.132 The Supreme Court, in an 8-1 decision, reversed their convictions.132 The Court held that the students were exercising their First Amendment rights "in their most pristine and classic form" and that the state could not "make criminal the peaceful expression of unpopular views".135 The decision underscored that peaceful protest cannot be shut down simply because it attracts a crowd or might provoke a hostile reaction from onlookers.136

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Snyder v. Phelps **, 562 U.S. 443 (2011)**
section.para: In a highly controversial 8-1 decision, the Court provided powerful protection for speech in a public forum, even when that speech is widely seen as outrageous and hurtful. Members of the Westboro Baptist Church picketed the funeral of a U.S. Marine, displaying signs with messages like "Thank God for Dead Soldiers" to convey their belief that God is punishing the U.S. for its tolerance of homosexuality.138 The marine's father sued and won a multi-million dollar verdict for intentional infliction of emotional distress. The Supreme Court reversed the verdict, holding that the picketing was speech on a matter of public concern in a public place and was therefore protected by the First Amendment.139 The Court reasoned, "As a Nation we have chosen... to protect even hurtful speech on public issues to ensure that we do not stifle public debate".139

title.emphasis: C. The Right to Petition: Litigation as Political Expression

section.para: The right to petition the government for a redress of grievances is one of the oldest constitutional liberties. The Supreme Court has interpreted this right to include not only direct lobbying but also accessing the courts and administrative agencies.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
NAACP v. Button **, 371 U.S. 415 (1963)**
section.para: Virginia enacted a statute that expanded the definition of improper solicitation of legal business, which had the effect of hampering the NAACP's strategy of sponsoring litigation to challenge segregation laws.141 The Supreme Court struck down the statute, holding that the NAACP's activities were "modes of expression and association protected by the First and Fourteenth Amendments".141 The Court recognized that for a group like the NAACP, litigation was not merely a means of resolving private disputes but was a form of political expression and a crucial tool for seeking political change, especially when other avenues like voting were blocked.141

section.title:
Case Analysis:
California Motor Transport Co. v. Trucking Unlimited **, 404 U.S. 508 (1972)**
section.para: This case established an important exception to the right to petition. A group of trucking companies alleged that their competitors had conspired to monopolize the industry by instituting baseless and repetitive legal and administrative proceedings to resist and defeat their applications for operating rights.142 The Supreme Court held that while the right to petition extends to all departments of the government, it does not protect "sham" activities that are not a genuine attempt to influence government action but are instead a pretext to directly interfere with a competitor's business.144 Such conduct, the Court ruled, is not immunized from antitrust laws.143
